title: Life Without Numbers
subtitle: 没有数字的语言：它如何改变人的思维

paragraphs.en: Numbers do not exist in all cultures. There are numberless hunter-gatherers in Amazonia, living along branches of the world's largest river tree. Instead of using words for precise quantities, these people rely exclusively on terms similar to "a few" or "some."
paragraphs.zh: 数字并非存在于所有文化中。在亚马孙地区，沿着世界上最大水系的分支，生活着没有数字概念的狩猎采集者。他们不使用表示精确数量的词语，而是完全依赖类似“几个”或“一些”的表述。

paragraphs.en: In contrast, our own lives are governed by numbers. As you read this, you are likely aware of what time it is, how old you are, your checking account balance, your weight and so on. The exact numbers we think with impact everything in our lives.
paragraphs.zh: 相比之下，我们的生活被数字所支配。当你阅读这段文字时，你很可能清楚现在几点、自己多大年纪、银行账户余额和体重等等。我们用以思考的精确数字影响着生活中的方方面面。

paragraphs.en: But, in a historical sense, number-conscious people like us are the unusual ones. For the bulk of our species' approximately 200,000-year lifespan, we had no means of precisely representing quantities. What's more, the 7,000 or so languages that exist today vary dramatically in how they utilize numbers.
paragraphs.zh: 但从历史角度来看，像我们这样有数字意识的人才是异类。在人类约20万年历史的绝大部分时间里，我们都没有精确表示数量的方法。而且，当今存在的约7000种语言在使用数字的方式上差异巨大。

paragraphs.en: Speakers of anumeric, or numberless, languages offer a window into how the invention of numbers reshaped the human experience. Cultures without numbers, or with only one or two precise numbers, include the Munduruku and Pirahã in Amazonia. Researchers have also studied some adults in Nicaragua who were never taught number words.
paragraphs.zh: 使用无数字语言的人为我们提供了一个窗口，让我们得以窥见数字的发明如何重塑了人类的体验。没有数字或仅有一两个精确数字的文化包括亚马孙地区的蒙杜鲁库人和皮拉罕人。研究人员还研究过尼加拉瓜一些从未学过数词的成年人。

paragraphs.en: Without numbers, healthy human adults struggle to precisely distinguish and recall quantities as low as four. In an experiment, a researcher will place nuts into a can one at a time and then remove them one by one. The person watching is asked to signal when all the nuts have been removed.
paragraphs.zh: 没有数字，健康的成年人也难以精确区分和记住低至四的数量。在一项实验中，研究人员将坚果逐一放入罐中，然后再逐一取出。观察者被要求在所有坚果被取出时发出信号。

paragraphs.en: Responses suggest that anumeric people have some trouble keeping track of how many nuts remain in the can, even if there are only four or five in total. This and many other experiments have led to a simple conclusion: When people do not have number words, they struggle to make quantitative distinctions that probably seem natural to someone like you or me.
paragraphs.zh: 结果表明，无数字语言使用者在追踪罐中剩余坚果数量方面存在困难，即便总共只有四五个。这项实验以及许多其他实验得出了一个简单的结论：当人们没有数词时，他们难以做出对你我而言似乎很自然的数量区分。

paragraphs.en: While only a small portion of the world's languages are anumeric or nearly anumeric, they demonstrate that number words are not a human universal. It is worth stressing that these anumeric people are cognitively normal, well-adapted to the surroundings they have dominated for centuries.
paragraphs.zh: 虽然世界上只有少部分语言是无数字或接近无数字的，但它们证明了数词并非人类的普遍特征。值得强调的是，这些无数字语言使用者在认知上是正常的，并且很好地适应了他们已主导数个世纪的生存环境。

paragraphs.en: As a child, I spent some time living with anumeric people, the Pirahã who live along the banks of the black Maici River. Like other outsiders, I was continually impressed by their superior understanding of the ecology we shared. Yet numberless people struggle with tasks that require precise discrimination between quantities.
paragraphs.zh: 小时候，我曾与无数字语言的皮拉罕人一起生活过一段时间，他们住在黑色的麦奇河畔。和其他外来者一样，我不断被他们对我们共享生态环境的卓越理解所折服。然而，没有数字的人在需要精确区分数量的任务上却力不从心。

paragraphs.en: Perhaps this should be unsurprising. After all, without counting, how can someone tell whether there are, say, seven or eight coconuts in a tree? Such seemingly straightforward distinctions become blurry through numberless eyes.
paragraphs.zh: 这或许并不令人意外。毕竟，如果不会计数，一个人怎能分辨一棵树上究竟有七个还是八个椰子呢？这种看似简单的区分，在无数字语言使用者的眼中变得模糊不清。

paragraphs.en: This conclusion is echoed by work with anumeric children in industrialized societies. Prior to being spoon-fed number words, children can only approximately discriminate quantities beyond three. We must be handed the cognitive tools of numbers before we can consistently and easily recognize higher quantities.
paragraphs.zh: 在工业化社会中对无数字儿童的研究也印证了这一结论。在被灌输数词之前，儿童只能大致区分三以上的数量。我们必须先获得数字这一认知工具，才能稳定而轻松地识别更大的数量。

paragraphs.en: In fact, acquiring the exact meaning of number words is a painstaking process that takes children years. Initially, kids learn numbers much like they learn letters. They recognize that numbers are organized sequentially, but have little awareness of what each individual number means.
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，准确掌握数词的含义是一个艰辛的过程，需要花费孩子们数年时间。起初，孩子学习数字就像学习字母一样。他们知道数字是按顺序排列的，但对每个数字的具体含义却知之甚少。

paragraphs.en: With time, they start to understand that a given number represents a quantity greater by one than the number coming before it. This "successor principle" is part of the foundation of our numerical cognition, but requires extensive practice to understand.
paragraphs.zh: 随着时间的推移，他们开始理解一个给定的数字代表的数量比它前面的数字多一。这种“后继原则”是我们数字认知的基础之一，但需要大量练习才能掌握。

paragraphs.en: None of us, then, is really a "numbers person." We are not born to handle quantitative distinctions skillfully. In the absence of the cultural traditions that fill our lives with numbers from infancy, we would all struggle with even basic quantitative distinctions.
paragraphs.zh: 因此，我们当中没有人天生就是“数字达人”。我们并非生来就能熟练处理数量区分。如果没有从婴儿时期就用数字充斥我们生活的文化传统，我们所有人都会在最基本的数量区分上感到困难。

paragraphs.en: Number words and their written forms transform our quantitative reasoning as they are introduced into our cognitive experience by our parents, peers and school teachers. The process seems so normal that we sometimes think of it as a natural part of growing up, but it is not. Human brains come equipped with certain quantitative instincts that are refined with age, but these instincts are very limited.
paragraphs.zh: 数词及其书写形式在被父母、同伴和学校老师引入我们的认知体验后，改变了我们的数量推理能力。这个过程看起来如此平常，以至于我们有时认为它是成长的天然组成部分，但事实并非如此。人脑确实具备某些随年龄发展而完善的数量本能，但这些本能非常有限。

paragraphs.en: Compared with other mammals, our numerical instincts are not as remarkable as many assume. We even share some basic instinctual quantitative reasoning with distant non-mammalian relatives like birds. Indeed, work with some other species suggests they too can refine their quantitative thought if they are introduced to the cognitive power tools we call numbers.
paragraphs.zh: 与其他哺乳动物相比，我们的数字本能并不像许多人以为的那样出色。我们甚至与鸟类等远亲非哺乳动物共享一些基本的本能数量推理能力。事实上，对其他物种的研究表明，如果它们也被引入我们称为数字的认知利器，它们同样能提升自己的数量思维。

paragraphs.en: So, how did we ever invent "unnatural" numbers in the first place? The answer is, literally, at your fingertips. The bulk of the world's languages use base-10, base-20 or base-5 number systems.
paragraphs.zh: 那么，我们究竟是如何发明出这些“非自然”的数字的呢？答案就在你的指尖——这并非比喻。世界上大多数语言使用十进制、二十进制或五进制数字系统。

words.meaning: adv. 确实地，字面意义上地

paragraphs.en: That is, these smaller numbers are the basis of larger numbers. English is a base-10 or decimal language, as evidenced by words like 14 ("four" + "10") and 31 ("three" x "10" + "one"). We speak a decimal language because an ancestral tongue, Proto-Indo-European, was decimally based.
paragraphs.zh: 也就是说，这些较小的数字是更大数字的基础。英语是一种十进制语言，这从14（“四”+“十”）和31（“三”乘以“十”+“一”）等词的构成中可见一斑。我们使用十进制语言，是因为我们的祖先语言——原始印欧语——就是以十进制为基础的。

paragraphs.en: Proto-Indo-European was decimally oriented because, as in so many cultures, our ancestors' hands served as the gateway to the realization that "five fingers on one hand is the same as five fingers on the other." Such momentary thoughts were represented in words and passed down across generations. This is why the word "five" in many languages is derived from the word for "hand."
paragraphs.zh: 原始印欧语之所以以十进制为导向，是因为就像在许多文化中一样，我们祖先的双手是领悟“一只手的五根手指等于另一只手的五根手指”这一概念的入口。这种瞬间的领悟被转化为语言并代代相传。这就是为什么许多语言中“五”这个词源自“手”。

paragraphs.en: Most number systems, then, are the by-product of two key factors: the human capacity for language and our inclination for focusing on our hands and fingers. This manual fixation — an indirect by-product of walking upright on two legs — has helped yield numbers in most cultures, but not all.
paragraphs.zh: 因此，大多数数字系统是两个关键因素的副产品：人类的语言能力以及我们对双手和手指的关注倾向。这种对手的执着——直立行走的间接副产品——在大多数文化中促成了数字的产生，但并非全部。

paragraphs.en: Cultures without numbers also offer insight into the cognitive influence of particular numeric traditions. Consider what time it is. Your day is ruled by minutes and seconds, but these concepts are not real in any physical sense and are nonexistent to numberless people.
paragraphs.zh: 没有数字的文化也让我们得以洞察特定数字传统对认知的影响。想一想现在几点了。你的一天被分钟和秒钟所支配，但这些概念在物理意义上并非真实存在，对于没有数字的人来说更是完全不存在的。

paragraphs.en: Minutes and seconds are the verbal and written representations of an uncommon base-60 number system used in ancient Mesopotamia. They reside in our minds, numerical artifacts that not all humans inherit conceptually.
paragraphs.zh: 分和秒是古代美索不达米亚使用的一种罕见的六十进制数字系统的口头和书面表达。它们存在于我们的头脑中，是并非所有人类都能在概念上继承的数字产物。

paragraphs.en: Research on the language of numbers shows, more and more, that one of our species' key characteristics is tremendous linguistic and cognitive diversity. If we are to truly understand how much our cognitive lives differ cross-culturally, we must continually explore the depths of our species' linguistic diversity.
paragraphs.zh: 关于数字语言的研究越来越多地表明，我们物种的关键特征之一是巨大的语言和认知多样性。如果我们要真正理解不同文化间认知生活的差异有多大，就必须不断探索人类语言多样性的深层奥秘。
